title: Slab-built vase in air-dry clay
subtitle: A four-sided vase assembled from flat slab panels
excerpt: A slab-built vase is assembled from four flat wall panels and a base, forming a rectangular vessel that holds dried flowers, grasses, and branches. Air-dry clay is not waterproof, so a glass jar or water tube inside holds the stems. The geometric form is forgiving of small irregularities and looks deliberate when finished.
sourceNotes: Slab-building technique. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: -built vase is assembled from five flat panels: a base and four walls. The square geometry is easier to join accurately than a curved form. No kiln or wheel is needed. The finished vase holds dried flowers, tall grasses, or sculptural branches; for fresh flowers, set a narrow glass jar inside as a water reservoir.

attrs.body: Air-dry clay softens when in contact with water over time. Use a narrow glass jar or metal water tube inside the vase for fresh-flower stems. The clay exterior remains intact as long as water is contained.
attrs.title: For fresh flowers

items.name: Pottery rolling pin and 8 mm guide rails

content.text: Roll a large slab 8 mm thick. For a vase 10 cm square and 18 cm tall, cut: one base 10 x 10 cm, four walls 10 x 18 cm. Leave the panels on the canvas board, covered loosely with plastic, until they reach
: stiff enough to stand without bending.

While the panels are still at leather-hard but before assembly, press any desired texture into the surfaces: a stamp, a leaf, or dragged lines with a fork or comb. This is easier to do on flat panels than on an assembled vase.
Step 3: Assemble two walls to the base

to attach two opposite walls to the base first. Score the bottom edge of each wall and the corresponding edge of the base. Press firmly and blend the interior corners. Use books or tins on either side of the walls as support while the joins set.
Step 4: Attach the remaining two walls

Once the first pair of walls is firm, score and slip the side edges of the assembled walls and the edges of the remaining panels. Press the new walls into position so they close the gaps. Blend all interior corner seams. Reinforce each corner with a thin coil of clay pressed into the angle and blended flat.

Dry bone-dry over two to three days. Lightly sand the edges and any rough areas outdoors with 120-grit sandpaper. Seal with two coats of PVA diluted 1:1 with water. A flat-finish acrylic varnish gives the most natural-looking surface.
Taper the walls slightly outward toward the top for a more organic silhouette.
Cut an opening that is off-centre rather than square across the top: useful for angled floral arrangements.

items.fix: Score the cracked surface, apply slurry, press a coil into the crack, and blend. Dry slowly under plastic.
items.cause: The interior corner coil was not applied or blended thoroughly.
items.symptom: Corner joints crack open during drying

items.fix: Trim the top edge with the fettling knife and ruler once the vase is assembled and firm.
items.cause: Panels were cut without a ruler.
